questions.prompt: Which conditional form is used for general truths and scientific facts?

options.text: First conditional: if + present simple, will + base verb
Zero conditional: if + present simple, present simple
Second conditional: if + past simple, would + base verb
Zero conditional: if + past simple, present simple

questions.explanation: The zero conditional (if + present simple, present simple) expresses facts and general truths where the result always follows the condition.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a correctly formed first conditional?

options.text: If you will study hard, you will pass.
If you studied hard, you will pass.
If you study hard, you will pass.
If you study hard, you would pass.

questions.explanation: The first conditional uses the present simple in the if-clause (never "will") and will + base verb in the main clause.

questions.prompt: What does the second conditional express?

options.text: A real and likely future situation
A general scientific truth
An imaginary or hypothetical situation in the present or future
A completed past action

questions.explanation: The second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) describes situations that are unreal, imaginary, or unlikely right now.

questions.prompt: In formal/standard English, which form of "be" is used in the if-clause of a second conditional?

options.text: "was" for all persons
"is" for all persons
"were" for all persons
"are" for all persons

questions.explanation: In formal and standard English, the second conditional uses "were" for all persons in the if-clause: "If I were you, I would apologise."

questions.prompt: What does "unless" mean in a conditional sentence?

options.text: if
although
if ... not
even if

questions.explanation: "Unless" means "if ... not". For example, "Unless you hurry, you will miss the train" means "If you don't hurry, you will miss the train."
